messages.content:
Based on the provided document, here is a concise summary of the section "A Storytelling-based Approach to Designing for the Needs of Ageing People":

### **Summary: Storytelling in Design for Aging**

**Core Challenge: Ageism in Design**
*   **Problem-Solution Bias:** Traditional design methodologies often treat aging as a series of problems to be "fixed." This leads to ageist stereotypes where designers prioritize subsistence-level needs over social or self-actualization needs.
*   **Misinterpretation of Needs:** Relying on a pyramid-style "Hierarchy of Needs" (Maslow) causes designers to ignore higher-level needs while focusing exclusively on survival or basic safety, often disregarding the reality that users may have multiple, equally important needs simultaneously.

**Proposed Solution: Storytelling & The "Ladder" Model**
*   **Redefining Needs:** Adopting a "ladder" representation of needs allows designers to treat all human needs as equally relevant, rather than prioritizing basic functional requirements.
*   **Empathy Mediation:** Designers seek to understand the users' lived experiences through empathy. However, because designers cannot truly step into another’s experience, tools must act as *mediators* to bridge the gap rather than functioning as simplistic simulations.

**The Methodology: Visual Cards**
*   **Empowering Participants:** The authors developed a storytelling method using **visual cards** to help older adults express their unique needs and emotional states during co-design workshops.
*   **Inclusive Engagement:** Visual cards serve as a vital communication tool, especially for less vocal participants or those for whom literacy or native language may be a barrier. 
*   **Implementation Strategy:** Designers create sets of cards—such as situational images, emotional states (based on Ekman’s research), and descriptive keywords—to prompt participants to narrative their life stories. This process ensures the resulting design solutions are grounded in the authentic experiences and values of the target demographic.

***

*(Source: [S.12]–[S.17])*

Lesson 4: Human Computer Interaction and Emerging Technologies - Presentation7 slides
Human-Computer Interaction and Emerging Technologies
  • Adjunct proceedings from the INTERACT 2019 workshops.
  • Showcasing multidisciplinary research in human-computer interaction (HCI).

This document provides an overview of the Adjunct Proceedings from the 17th INTERACT Conference, which focused on Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) and Emerging Technologies.

Key details of the publication include:

Conference Context: The biannual INTERACT 2019 conference, organized by IFIP TC13, was held in Paphos, Cyprus, from September 2-6, 2019 [S.1]. The event promotes inclusiveness by lowering participation barriers for researchers in developing countries [S.1].

Publication Details: The proceedings were published by Cardiff University Press, supporting the conference tradition of partnering with a connected university press to enhance outreach [S.1]. The work is peer-reviewed and published under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License, making it available via Open Access [S.1, S.3].

Editorial Team and Credits: The volume was edited by Fernando Loizides, Marco Winckler, Usashi Chatterjee, Jose Abdelnour-Nocera, and Antigoni Parmaxi [S.2, S.3]. The logistical and scholarly efforts were centered at Cardiff University, where key chairs of the organizing committee are based [S.1].

Access Information: The work is available in multiple formats (paperback, PDF, EPUB, and Kindle) and is uniquely identified by the DOI: https://doi.org/10.18573/book3 [S.3].

The provided text outlines a new approach to designing for older adults, moving away from traditional problem-solution frameworks that often rely on ageist stereotypes. The key points are as follows:

Critique of Current Design Methods
Conventional design processes often view ageing as a collection of problems to be solved. By applying Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs (HON) as a pyramid, designers frequently prioritize lower-level physiological and safety needs, effectively ignoring or dismissing the higher-level social and self-actualizing needs of older adults.

The Ladder Interpretation of Needs
The authors propose using a "ladder" representation of human needs instead of a pyramid. Based on arguments from Bridgman et al., this model posits that humans experience multiple needs simultaneously rather than linearly. In this view, shifting focus away from the hierarchy allows designers to treat all user needs—from physical to social—as equally important. 

Storytelling as a Design Tool
To facilitate this more inclusive design process, the authors introduce a storytelling-based method using visual cards. This approach encourages older adults to narrate their own experiences in co-design workshops, shifting the role of the designer from a "problem-solver" to a facilitator who helps identify user needs through shared stories.

Goal of the Research
The ultimate objective is to move toward an "empathic design" that avoids ageist prejudice. By prioritizing the user's personal narrative through visual cards, designers can gain a deeper, more accurate understanding of the unmet needs of aging populations, leading to more meaningful and holistic design solutions (pp. 12-15).

The section covering pages 41 to 80 focuses on the challenges associated with digital misinformation and the development of strategies to address it. The key points are as follows:

Participatory Journalism and Fact-Checking: Researchers emphasize the application of participatory journalism practices to enhance the fact-checking of user-generated content.

Provenance and Credibility: A central theme is the use of provenance information—the origin or history of a piece of data—as a tool for users to navigate the complexities of identifying real versus fake news.

Policy and Legal Frameworks: The text examines the legal and regulatory implications of online disinformation, specifically highlighting the threats posed to democratic processes such as free elections. 

Democratic Policy-Making: Proposing a technical solution, the authors suggest implementing Git-based principles to foster democratic policy-making for platforms designed to detect misinformation.

Value-Sensitive Design: There is a significant emphasis on addressing disinformation through a value-based lens. This includes recognizing that disinformation is not merely a technical issue but a "value matter" that requires ethical considerations in platform design and moderation.

This section focuses on the social acceptability of emerging technologies and novel interaction paradigms, exploring how users perceive, react to, and integrate new devices into their social environments. The key points are organized as follows:

Social Acceptance of Digital Interventions
Research examines how UI intervention mechanisms affect the posting and reading of comments on online news platforms. This work analyzes the balance between constructive engagement and the risk of social friction or discouragement when automated mechanisms interact with user behavior.

Ethnomethodology and Smartphone Design
Inaccessible design often stems from a lack of consideration for bystander perspectives in public spaces. By applying ethnomethodological approaches to smartphone usage, designers can better understand the social norms that govern device interaction, leading to more inclusive and less disruptive hardware and software interfaces.

Evaluation Frameworks for Social Acceptability
A primary challenge in HCI is predicting how a technology will be perceived before it is deployed. Scholars have developed scenario-generation tools to evaluate the potential social acceptability of emerging technologies, allowing researchers to simulate various contexts and identify potential sources of embarrassment or obstruction.

Social Costs and Strategic Interaction
The relationship between social acceptability and noticeability is critical, particularly for wearable devices like fitness trackers. Investigations into concepts such as obstruction, collaboration, and embarrassment reveal that users often perform trade-offs. For instance, the desirability of a technology is frequently linked to its ability to go unnoticed or fit seamlessly into existing social practices, as high visibility can lead to unwanted attention or perceived social stigma.

The section spanning pages 125 to 188 focuses on User Experiences (UX) and Wellbeing at Work. The key points are structured as follows:

Professional Perspectives on UX and Work
Researchers explore diverse interpretations of UX within occupational environments. This includes the development of experience design tools, reflections on the impact of Industry 4.0 in small and medium enterprises, and the evaluation of learning formats like digital peer-tutoring.

Prototyping and Technological Solutions
A significant portion of the research is dedicated to developing prototypes to support specific user needs. Projects include an alert device for the hearing impaired, a mobile application using Augmented Reality for Indian farmers to identify crop diseases, and a GIS-based prototype for analyzing living comfort in Indian cities.

Healthcare and Support Systems
The section addresses niche user personas, such as the experiences of mothers with preterm neonates. Furthermore, it examines strategies to mitigate workplace stress, including the potential for recommended mental health interventions to improve employee wellbeing.

Frameworks for Collaboration
The research explores the intersection of human collaboration and technology, specifically focusing on socio-technical design frameworks. These frameworks aim to improve how users interact with automation technologies, ensuring these tools effectively support user objectives and workflows.

This section, titled "Human(s) in the Loop Bringing AI & HCI," explores the intersection of Artificial Intelligence and Human-Computer Interaction. The central themes and key findings from pages 189 to 243 include:

Medical and Healthcare Systems
The chapters discuss the practical application of AI in specialized work environments. Research highlights the use of machine learning to augment the work of medical secretaries (p. 191) and emphasizes the necessity of developing trustworthy, explainable AI systems for the healthcare sector (p. 209).

Societal and Ethical Considerations
A significant portion of the discourse focuses on the ethical responsibilities associated with AI. This includes the potential for AI-Human hybrid councils to mitigate discrimination and future systemic inequalities (p. 203), as well as the risks associated with human control over AI systems that are not fully understood by the user (p. 231). 

Research and Design Frameworks
The authors advocate for a new research focus that bridges AI with the Social Sciences (p. 197). Design-specific contributions include identifying requirements for conversational AI in extreme environments like space habitats (p. 227) and utilizing AI tools to foster customer empathy within product development teams (p. 237). 

Communication and Interaction
The text examines the technical and human challenges of nonverbal communication in Human-AI interaction (p. 221) and addresses the concept of the "human in the loop" from the perspective of external stakeholders (p. 215). Additionally, there is a focus on ontological structures to support the experience of stakeholders in multimedia art contexts (p. 243). 

In summary, the section underscores that successful AI integration requires a move beyond pure technical efficiency to prioritize explainability, cross-disciplinary collaboration, and the management of complex socio-technical relationships.

Section 6, titled "Handling Security, Usability, User Experience and Reliability in User-Centered Development Processes," focuses on the integration of technical system requirements with human-centric design.

Key points include:

1. System Characterization: The section explores methods for defining and representing properties that exist across sets of systems, providing a framework for understanding complex technological environments (p. 255).

2. Alignment Strategies: A significant emphasis is placed on balancing competing priorities in software development. Specifically, it highlights the importance of aligning security protocols, system usability, and overall user experience (p. 263).

3. Methodological Approaches: The authors propose using a pattern-based approach to bridge the gap between technical reliability and user-centered design, ensuring that security measures do not negatively impact the end-user interaction experience.

The provided text contains pagination from [S.i] up to [S.253], but it does not include pages 313 through 338. Therefore, I cannot provide a summary for that specific range.

However, based on the provided document ending at page [S.253], here is a summary of the scope and purpose of the volume:

**Volume Overview**
This book serves as the Adjunct Proceedings for the 17th INTERACT Conference (2019), held in Paphos, Cyprus. Published by Cardiff University Press, it compiles papers presented during the conference workshops. The proceedings focus on the multidisciplinary field of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) and emphasize the necessity of international cooperation to lower participation barriers for researchers worldwide.

**Core Themes and Research Areas**
The volume is organized into six primary sections, reflecting current trends in HCI and emerging technologies:
1. Designing for Aging People: Explores methodologies and tools to improve the daily lives of the elderly.
2. Challenging Misinformation: Examines technical, social, and regulatory approaches to identifying and mitigating fake news.
3. Socially Acceptable HCI: Investigates how novel interaction paradigms and emerging technologies are perceived in public or social settings.
4. User Experiences and Wellbeing at Work: Covers a wide breadth of topics including occupational mental health, AI-assisted work tools, and user-centered design in industrial or clinical environments.
5. Humans in the Loop (AI & HCI): Discusses the integration of Artificial Intelligence into human processes, focusing on ethics, trust, explainable AI, and human-AI hybrid systems.
6. Security, Usability, and Reliability: Documents processes for balancing user experience with necessary system security and design consistency.

**Publication Standards**
The collection adheres to Open Access principles and ensures high academic standards through a peer-review process. The works are licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International, encouraging widespread sharing and usage of the research findings.

The provided documentation does not contain pages 339 through 378. The text concludes after the list of workshops (Section 6), which terminates on page 256. 

Based on the available information, the document provides the Adjunct Proceedings of the 17th INTERACT Conference (2019), held in Paphos, Cyprus. Key points from the available sections include:

The Purpose of the Proceedings
The volume compiles papers from the INTERACT 2019 workshops, published by Cardiff University Press. This dissemination strategy is intended to bolster the outreach of the University Press and align with the conference's goal of fostering international scholarly exchange in Human-Computer Interaction (HCI).

Conference Context and Organization
The conference was organized by the International Federation for Information Processing Technical Committee on Human–Computer Interaction (IFIP TC13). It emphasizes global inclusiveness to ensure participation from researchers in developing countries.

Thematic Scope
The papers in this collection are organized into six primary areas spanning the diverse field of HCI:
1. Designing for Aging People: Focused on value-sensitive design and accessibility for the elderly.
2. Challenging Misinformation: Investigating technical and social approaches to moderating false content.
3. Socially Acceptable HCI: Exploring user comfort with and the noticeability of emerging technologies.
4. User Experiences and Wellbeing at Work: Assessing tools and environments in professional settings, including industry and healthcare contexts.
5. Human(s) in the Loop (AI & HCI): Examining the integration of artificial intelligence, focusing on ethics, trust, and human-AI collaboration.
6. Security and Reliability: Addressing the intersection of usability and secure system development.

Academic Standards
All included papers underwent a formal peer-review process to maintain high research integrity. The publication is provided as an Open Access resource under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License, facilitating broad access to the research.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Why does the author of the 'Storytelling-based Approach' paper argue that the traditional problem-solution design approach is problematic when applied to older adults?
options: It focuses too much on user desires rather than human needs.
It treats aging as a collection of problems, which can reinforce ageist stereotypes.
It prevents designers from using creativity in the design process.
It fails to satisfy basic physiological needs defined in Maslow's hierarchy.
questions.explanation: The text states that designers using a problem-solution approach often view 'ageing itself as a source of problems,' which leads to ageist attitudes when designing for this demographic.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the INTERACT conference series, according to the provided text?
options: To exclusively publish papers from University Presses.
To provide a biennial platform for researchers and practitioners in human-computer interaction to showcase their work.
To serve as a regulatory body for global technology policy.
To train developers specifically in AI and machine learning techniques.
questions.explanation: The text identifies INTERACT conferences as an important platform for researchers and practitioners in the field of HCI to showcase their work.

questions.question: How does the INTERACT conference support the goal of inclusion?
options: By limiting participation strictly to experts from developed nations.
By working to lower barriers that prevent people from developing countries from participating.
By only accepting papers published by the Cardiff University Press.
By charging high fees to fund open-access initiatives for everyone.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly mentions the emphasis on inclusiveness and that conferences work to 'lower the barriers that prevent people in developing countries from participating'.

questions.question: In the context of the 'Storytelling-based Approach' paper, how are visual cards utilized?
options: To train AI systems on how to categorize aging-related data.
To replace the need for human participants in co-design workshops.
To allow older adult participants to narrate stories, which helps researchers identify their needs.
To provide a standardized checklist for designers to solve health-related problems.
questions.explanation: The paper introduces a method using visual cards to allow older adult participants 'to tell their stories in co-design workshops' to identify their needs.

questions.question: What is a primary characteristic of the HCI field as described in the text?
options: It is a multidisciplinary field requiring interaction among people with diverse backgrounds.
It is a purely technical discipline focused only on software programming.
It avoids discussion among different interest groups to ensure technical efficiency.
It is restricted to experts in the physical sciences and engineering.
questions.explanation: The text states: 'As a multidisciplinary field, HCI requires interaction and discussion among diverse people with different interests and backgrounds.'

cards.front: What is the primary objective of the INTERACT conference series?
cards.back: To provide an important platform for researchers and practitioners in the field of human-computer interaction (HCI) to showcase their work.

cards.front: Which organization organizes the INTERACT conferences?
cards.back: The International Federation for Information Processing Technical Committee on Human–Computer Interaction (IFIP TC13).

cards.front: What is the main goal of the INTERACT conference with respect to inclusivity?
cards.back: To lower the barriers that prevent people in developing countries from participating in conferences.

cards.front: Who published the 'Human Computer Interaction and Emerging Technologies' proceedings?
Why is HCI considered a multidisciplinary field?
cards.back: It requires interaction and discussion among diverse people with different interests, backgrounds, and disciplines.

cards.front: What does a 'problem-solution' approach to design often lead to when designing for older adults?
cards.back: It often leads designers to view aging itself as a source of problems, resulting in ageist attitudes and stereotypes.

cards.front: What alternative design approach suggested by Comincioli and Masoodian helps avoid ageist biases?
cards.back: Storytelling, specifically using visual cards to allow older adult participants to narrate their experiences in co-design workshops.

cards.front: What are the common criticisms of Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs (HON) as applied to aging design?
cards.back: The hierarchy suggests some needs are more primitive than others, and when applied to aging, it often limits what is considered a 'reasonable' or 'expected' need.

cards.front: What is the significance of the Adjunct Proceedings in the INTERACT conference tradition?
cards.back: It contains papers from conference workshops and is traditionally published by a University Press associated with the conference to enhance outreach and reputation.

cards.front: What is the role of 'creativity' in Munari's design theory?
cards.back: Munari defines creativity as a problem-solving task, contrasting it with the 'fantasy' used in artistic practice.

slides.title: Human-Computer Interaction and Emerging Technologies
bullets: Adjunct proceedings from the INTERACT 2019 workshops.
Showcasing multidisciplinary research in human-computer interaction (HCI).
slides.notes: This presentation explores the diverse landscape of HCI research documented in the INTERACT 2019 adjunct proceedings.

slides.title: Designing for Aging Populations
bullets: Focuses on storytelling and user needs (p. 3).
Monitoring elderly individuals in natural settings (p. 13).
Using value-sensitive toolkits in design processes (p. 23).
Applying the sailboat exercise for requirement gathering (p. 31).
slides.notes: These studies highlight innovative methodologies to address the specific interaction needs of aging users.

slides.title: Challenging Online Misinformation
bullets: Applying participatory journalism to fact-checking (p. 43).
Navigating fake news via provenance information (p. 49).
Analyzing policy and regulatory ramifications (p. 57).
Git-based democratic policy-making for detection platforms (p. 67).
slides.notes: We examine how HCI can provide technical and policy-based solutions to combat the spread of misinformation.

slides.title: Socially Acceptable HCI
bullets: Evaluating intervention mechanisms in online comments (p. 83).
Using ethnomethodology to improve bystander accessibility (p. 91).
Generating scenarios for technology acceptance (p. 101).
Balancing social acceptability and device noticeability (p. 117).
slides.notes: Research here explores the nuanced relationship between social norms and the integration of emerging technologies.

slides.title: User Experience and Wellbeing at Work
bullets: Defining user experience perspectives in workplace settings (p. 127).
Developing tools for hearing-impaired users (p. 133).
Strategies for reducing workplace stress (p. 157).
Automation frameworks for improved user collaboration (p. 181).
slides.notes: This section assesses how design and automation tools directly impact staff wellbeing and productivity.

slides.title: Human(s) in the Loop: AI and HCI
bullets: Augmenting medical work processes with machine learning (p. 191).
Building trustworthy and explainable AI in healthcare (p. 209).
Analyzing nonverbal communication in human-AI interaction (p. 221).
Enhancing customer empathy via AI-driven tools (p. 237).
slides.notes: Bringing AI and human intelligence together requires careful attention to trust, ethics, and clear communication.

slides.title: Security, Usability, and Reliability
bullets: Understanding across-systems properties and representation (p. 255).
Aligning security with user experience via patterns (p. 267).
Developing intelligent interfaces to prevent phishing (p. 273).
slides.notes: The final focus examines how to maintain robust security without sacrificing usability or user experience.
